Figuring it’s better not to take any chances, you set the controls for the door to Sam’s room so that it can only be opened from the outside. You don’t want her waking up while you’re flying around and doing anything crazy. With that done, you head to the controls of your ship and begin the process of flying back to Tavros. Once you’ve finished the launching protocols and been cleared by the control tower, you set the ship to auto-pilot and open up your codex to check the message you received. It’s a very business-like explanation of the collar that Sam has been fitted with — there’s even an official manual included as an attachment. The manual boasts that the Carver Consolidated Organic Lifeform Pacification Device is part of a class of ‘humane’ restraining devices known as ‘chemical restraints’, so named because the devices administer chemicals to the wearer to encourage, discourage, or outright force (or prevent) whatever behavior the controller of the device wishes while monitoring the wearer’s vitals in order to automatically avoid overdose. The message from Gastigoth informs you that this particular collar has been equipped with sedatives, stimulants, aphrodisiacs, birth control, and fertility enhancers; additionally, it can be controlled by the attuned remote, by voice, or in response to specific actions on the wearer’s part, such as attempting to remove the collar themselves. Other triggering conditions may be set via synchronized data slate, with anything from location, to the wearer’s heart rate, to specific times of day. Extensive health and vital information about the wearer can also be monitored from a data slate, including pregnancy — which the manual is quick to explain will not be at risk from any officially-provided or sponsored drugs the collar can be filled with. It seems you have quite the impressive little piece of technology here!

I just remember waking up in a police cruiser. You weren’t mentioned at the trial or sentencing either. I can recall almost all of my time at Gastigoth, but those damn sedatives they use make it so you don’t really remember anything while you’re on them, so I don’t clearly remember meeting anyone in the breeding program.” The ausar looks away from you again before muttering, “I’m not even really sure how I got where I am right now. Or even where I am at all...” She might have been a hardened pirate, but looking at Sam as she is now, vulnerable and bereft of guile, she just seems like a scared young woman. You would’ve expected her to at least act surly or even angry with you, but instead she seems resigned to whatever fate you have in store for her, if incredibly anxious about it. You skip right over it though and reassure her, letting her know you’re interested only in the aphrodisiacs, if you even intend to use her collar at all. The dark-skinned ausar retreats into her shell again, looking tense and fearful once more. “Umm, about that. I know if you use those, I’m gonna get really baby-crazy. You shouldn’t listen to me though. That’s just the heat talking! I don’t want to have my pups end up being raised in Gastigoth or something like- uh, I just don’t want them there.” There’s obviously something serious in that cut-off sentence, but you don’t pry just yet. Plenty of time for that later. Instead you tell Sam of the nursery your father left for you on Tavros, and how it has everything a parent could ask for for their children. You would never send your children to be raised in a corporate prison, especially when you have a much better alternative. Sam’s blue eyes widen as your explanation goes on, and relief washes over her. She finally lets one of her blonde-furred legs stretch out in front of her as she sighs out much of her tension. “Haaa... That’s a relief. Now I don’t have to worry so much if you knock me up. Sam doesn’t dignify your comment with a response, but she doesn’t deny your words. Before the ensuing silence can grow too awkward, you tell her you should be arriving at Tavros soon, so you need to prepare for docking protocols. In a tone that shows she already knows the answer to the question, Sam asks, “Am I going to be allowed to leave the ship when you do?” [pc.nmh |You shake your head ruefully and apologize. |You shake your head ruefully and apologize. |You scoff at her question and tell her you’re not taking any chances. ] “Okay,” Sam mutters defeatedly. The canid girl rolls over on her side with her back to you, meekly accepting her situation and curling her bushy blonde tail around herself again defensively. You make your departure, locking the door to her room behind you before returning to the cockpit. You’ll have to give Sam some time to adjust to her new surroundings and suss out how much you can trust her in the coming days.

original: The station’s visitor lobby is the most colorful place you’ve seen so far: it has two potted plants: little bonzai trees on either end of a glass end table that’s stacked with datapads and holomags. A pair of curved silver couches surround it, giving you a place to relax while you wait... though the atmosphere is somewhat blemished by the half-dozen armed guards in full armor and jump jets standing around the north access tunnel. Sitting up against one of the walls is a large holoterminal with the words “Penal Station Services” pulsating slowly, constantly drawing your eye from anywhere in the room. Aside from the way you got here, back east, there’s a single elevator door on the western bulkhead. It’s marked “Controller’s Deck,” and has no visible access buttons.

original:
The elevator opens directly into Warden Kasmiran’s office: a huge, cavernous audience chamber as much reminiscent of a medieval throne room as a modern executive’s suit. The walls are dominated by holographic security displays intermixed with handsome art-nouveau styled paintings of her fellow cylirians and their ashen homeworld. The Warden’s desk sits on a raised dias, a semi-circle of pure marble that clashes horribly with the gunmetal gray of the bulkheads and deck.

Several bonzai trees surround the edges, separating you and her seat of power with a wall of green and browns. The rest of the desk is dominated by dozens of small holoscreens, all facing inwards to the Warden’s seat, and behind her is a huge projector screen with cylirian symbols scrawling across it too fast to translate.

Unlike everywhere else in the station, you don’t have guns constantly aimed at you in here. In fact, there’s no security measure to be seen - beyond the Warden herself, an imposing figure leaning back in her chair, one leg crossed over the other. A thin cigar is clutched in her latex fingertips, wisping into the air vent directly over her head, and you cannot avoid the feeling of her golden-red cyber eyes drilling into you, unblinking and unreadable.
